12.1 An Overview of Glycolysis

Overview of Carbohydrate Catabolic and Anabolic Processes
The red arrows show the breakdown of sugars via the glycolytic pathway to generate A T P. The blue arrows show the gluconeogenesis pathway, including the synthesis of polysaccharides such as glycogen. The green arrow shows the pentose phosphate pathway, needed for nucleotide synthesis
Figure 12.1 Catabolic and anabolic processes in anaerobic carbohydrate metabolism.

Phases of Glycolysis (1 of 2)
Glycolysis has 10 reactions, which can be divided into two distinct phases: one to invest energy and one to generate energy
In the energy-investment phase, two A T Ps are consumed to break down glucose into two triose phosphates
Figure 12.2 The two phases of glycolysis and the products of glycolysis.

Phases of Glycolysis (2 of 2)
In the energy-generation phase, the triose phosphates are oxidized to two pyruvic acids, thereby producing four A T P and two N A D H
Overall:
are
converted to 2 pyruvic acid
Figure 12.2 The two phases of glycolysis and the products of glycolysis.

Aerobic and Anaerobic Fates of Glycolysis (1 of 2)
N A D H may be oxidized to
during cellular respiration
by the electron transport chain, which generates a proton-motive force to drive the A T P-producing A T P synthase complex (aerobic fate)
N A D H may also be converted to
by transfer of
electrons (and protons) to an organic molecule used as electron acceptor (anaerobic fate)
two common anaerobic fates are homolactic fermentation and alcoholic fermentation

Aerobic and Anaerobic Fates of Glycolysis (2 of 2)

First Reaction – Hexokinase

Third Reaction – Phosphofructokinase (P F K)
P F K is an allosteric enzyme and a major control point for glycolysis

Fourth Reaction – Aldolase
Aldolase cleaves fructose 1,6-bisphosphate (F1,6B P) into two triose phosphates in a reversible reaction. Although

Figure 12.6 Reaction mechanism for glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase.
The reaction involves a two-electron transfer (onto

of −49.4 kJ/mol.

Seventh Reaction – Phosphoglycerate Kinase
This is a substrate-level phosphorylation.
The energy of oxidation of an aldehyde to a carboxylic acid is conserved in the form of A T P through these two reactions
Eighth Reaction – Phosphoglycerate Mutase

Formation of a Diphosphorylated Intermediate
The enzyme contains a phosphohistidine residue in its active site

Ninth Reaction – Enolase
This is an α,β-elimination. The elimination product is

Tenth Reaction – Pyruvate Kinase (P K)
This is the second glycolytic substrate-level phosphorylation

P K Mechanism
Although the synthesis of A T P is an endergonic step, the overall reaction is exergonic because of the spontaneous tautomerization of the enolpyruvate to the more stable keto form (pyruvate)

12.3 Metabolic Fates of Pyruvate

Anaerobic Fates of Pyruvate
N A D H must be reoxidized to
for glycolysis to continue
Oxidation in mitochondria cannot keep up with the N A D H produced by high rates of glycolysis, and anaerobic organisms lack mitochondria

Balanced equation for homolactic fermentation:
Balanced equation for alcoholic fermentation:
No net change in oxidation state for each process

Thermodynamic Outcome of Anaerobic Glycolysis (2 of 2)
Both equations involve an exergonic process coupled to an endergonic process:
The efficiency of this process is 64.4/228 = 28.2% under standard conditions; the remainder of the free energy ensures the reaction goes to completion
12.5 Gluconeogenesis

Glucose Synthesis and Use (1 of 2)
The human brain requires 120 grams/day of glucose out of 160 needed by the entire body
Glycogen reserves can provide 190 g and glucose in body fluids is ~20 g; hence, the body contains about one day’s supply
When glucose is depleted (fasting or prolonged exercise), glucose must be synthesized from other sources, such as gluconeogenesis

Glucose Synthesis and Use (2 of 2)
Gluconeogenesis means (literally) synthesis of new glucose
Glucose is synthesized from non-carbohydrate precursors
Gluconeogenesis is like glycolysis in reverse, but three “irreversible” reactions must be reversed
Figure 12.9 Synthesis and use of glucose in the human body.

Glycolysis and Gluconeogenesis (1 of 2)

12.6 Coordinated Regulation of Glycolysis and Gluconeogenesis

Control of Glucose Breakdown and Synthesis
Glycolysis and gluconeogenesis must be controlled reciprocally to prevent futile cycles
Regulation must also take into account the need to maintain pools of intermediates for other biosynthetic purposes
Figure 12.13 Substrate cycles in glycolysis/gluconeogenesis.

Control Points (1 of 3)
Figure 12.14 identifies the major allosteric activators and inhibitors of the key exergonic reactions in glycolysis and gluconeogenesis.

Control Points (2 of 3)
Figure 12.14 identifies the major allosteric activators and inhibitors of the key exergonic reactions in glycolysis and gluconeogenesis.

Control Points (3 of 3)
Figure 12.14 identifies the major allosteric activators and inhibitors of the key exergonic reactions in glycolysis and gluconeogenesis.

Pasteur Effect
Louis Pasteur (1822–1895) observed that anaerobic yeast exposed to oxygen caused the rate of glucose utilization to decrease
Intracellular levels of glycolytic intermediates after oxygenation pointed to the conversion of F6P to F1,6B P as an important control point
Phosphofructokinase (P F K) was found to be inhibited by A T P and activated by A D P and A M P
Many experiments suggested that glycolysis depends on adenylate energy charge

Allosteric Regulation of P F K
Figure 12.15 Allosteric control of liver phosphofructokinase and structure of a bacterial homolog.

Role of Fructose 2,6-Bisphosphate (F2,6B P)
F2,6B P activates glycolysis and inhibits gluconeogenesis. F2,6B P is synthesized by the bifunctional P F K-2/F B  Pase-2, which is distinct from the enzymes found in glycolysis and gluconeogenesis

Allosteric Regulation at the Pyruvate Kinase – Pyruvate Carboxylase/P E P Carboxykinase Substrate Cycle
Figure 12.15 Allosteric control of liver phosphofructokinase and structure of a bacterial homolog.
12.7 Entry of Other Sugar into the Glycolytic Pathway

Utilization of Sugars Other Than Glucose
Figure 12.19 Routes for utilizing substrates other than glucose in glycolysis.

Glycosidic Bond Cleavage of Disaccharides
Dietary polysaccharides are metabolized by hydrolysis to monosaccharides
Intracellular carbohydrate stores, such as glycogen, are mobilized as phosphorylated monosaccharides by phosphorolysis
Figure 12.20 Cleavage of a glycosidic bond by hydrolysis or phosphorolysis.

α-amylase in saliva cleaves
linkages from the
nonreducing ends, but cannot
cleave the
linkage at a branch point

12.8 Glycogen Metabolism in Muscle and Liver

Glycogen Utilization in Cells (1 of 2)
Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves
bonds via
phosphorolysis, yielding α-d-glucose-1-phosphate

Glycogen Utilization in Cells (2 of 2)
In order to enter glycolysis or other pathways, α-d-glucose-1-phosphate is then converted into α-d-glucose-6-phosphate by phosphoglucomutase

The Debranching Process in Glycogen Catabolism (1 of 2)
The bifunctional
(a debranching enzyme) catalyzes two reactions:
The transferase activity transfers three of the remaining four glucose residues from the limit branch to another nonreducing end via a new
linkage
The
activity removes the
remaining glucose molecule

The Debranching Process in Glycogen Catabolism (2 of 2)
Figure 12.22 The debranching process in glycogen catabolism.

Synthesis of Glycogen from U D P-Glucose (1 of 2)
Figure 12.23 Pathway for the conversion of glucose monomers to polymeric glycogen.
U D P-glucose is an activated form of glucose for glycogen synthesis

Synthesis of Glycogen from U D P-Glucose (2 of 2)
Figure 12.24 The glycogen synthase reaction.
Using U D P-glucose, α(1→4)-linked glycogen is synthesized by glycogen synthase

The Glycogen Branching Process
Figure 12.25 The branching process in glycogen synthesis.
Glycogen branches are added by an amylo-(1,4→1,6) transglycosylase, which transfers 6 to 7 residues from a branch terminus at least 11 residues long
12.9 Coordinated Regulation of Glycogen Metabolism

Controlled Synthesis and Breakdown of Glycogen (1 of 3)
Hormone binds a cell surface receptor, releasing a G-protein which activates adenylate cyclase
Figure 12.26 The regulatory cascade controlling glycogen breakdown.

Controlled Synthesis and Breakdown of Glycogen (2 of 3)
Adenylate cyclase synthesizes cA M P, which binds the regulatory subunit of protein kinase A (P K A), releasing the active C subunit of P K A
Figure 12.26 The regulatory cascade controlling glycogen breakdown.

Controlled Synthesis and Breakdown of Glycogen (3 of 3)
Active P K A phospho-rylates phosphorylase b kinase, activating it
Active kinase converts inactive phosphorylase b to the active phos-phorylase a, catalyzing glycogen breakdown
Figure 12.26 The regulatory cascade controlling glycogen breakdown.

Activation of P K A by cA M P
Figure 12.27 Activation of cyclic A M P-dependent protein kinase (protein kinase A) by cA M P.

Figure 12.30 Control of glycogen phosphorylase activity.
In the unphosphorylated form, the phosphorylase exists primarily in the less active T-state. The phosphorylated form exists

Control of Glycogen SynthaseActivity (1 of 2)
The enzyme in its unphosphorylated a form is active
Several kinases may act to phosphorylate glycogen synthase converting it to the inactive b form
Figure 12.31 Control of glycogen synthase activity.

Control of Glycogen SynthaseActivity (2 of 2)
Dephosphorylation reactivates the enzyme
Glucose-6-phosphate acts as an allosteric activator to shift the equilibrium of the phosphorylated form from the T to the R state
Figure 12.31 Control of glycogen synthase activity.

12.10 A Biosynthetic Pathway That Oxidizes Glucose: The Pentose Phosphate Pathway

The Metabolic Requirement of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway (P P P)
The P P P is mainly needed to provide:
reducing equivalents (in the form of N A D P H) for reductive biosynthesis
ribose-5-phosphate (R5P) for nucleotide and nucleic acid synthesis

The Four Stages of P P P
Two oxidations (glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase) in stage 1 generate N A D P H and
Ribose 5-phosphate is produced in stage 2
Figure 12.32 Overall strategy of the pentose phosphate pathway.

The Oxidative Phase of P P P
and 1
pentose phosphate are generated from 1 glucose 6-phosphate and
Figure 12.33 Oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway.

The Nonoxidative Phase of P P P (1 of 2)
The phosphopentose isomerase reaction
Ribulose 5-phosphate can also be converted to the pentose sugar xylulose 5-phosphate (X5P) by ribulose 5-phosphate epimerase.

The Nonoxidative Phase of P P P (2 of 2)
Transketolase and transaldolase reactions
R5P (a C5 sugar) and X5P (C5) are converted to glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate (G A P; C3) and sedoheptulose 7-phosphate (S7P; C7) by transketolase, which are further metabolized to fructose 6-phosphate (F6P; C6) and erythrose 4-phosphate (E4P; a C4 sugar) by transaldolase
Moreover, E4P and X5P can be altered to G A P and F6P in a second transketolase reaction

Modes of P P P
Figure 12.36 Alternative pentose phosphate pathway modes.

Chapter 12 Summary (1 of 3)
Glycolysis is a central pathway to extract energy from carbohydrates
it comprises of ten reactions that can be divided into two distinct phases (energy-investment and energy-generation phases)
it catabolizes glucose into pyruvate, yielding energy in form of A T P and N A D H
Glycolysis can proceed under anaerobic conditions as fermentation, or under aerobic conditions as cellular respiration
however, much more energy is generated under aerobic conditions

Chapter 12 Summary (2 of 3)
The major sites of regulation for glycolysis are the reactions catalyzed by phosphofructokinase, pyruvate kinase, and hexokinase
regulation is related to cellular energy needs and occurs via allosteric mechanisms
Gluconeogenesis is the synthesis of glucose from noncarbohydrate precursors
seven glycolytic enzymes are also used in gluconeogenesis, but three glycolytic enzymes are replaced by four specific ones to ensure thermodynamic favorability

Chapter 12 Summary (3 of 3)
Regulation of glycolysis and gluconeogenesis is highly coordinated
fructose 2,6-bisphosphate is the key regulator
Glycogen is the intracellular storage polysaccharide in animals
glycogen phosphorylase is the rate-limiting step in glycogen breakdown, while glycogen synthase is the rate-limiting step in glycogen synthesis
The pentose phosphate pathway is an alternative glucose oxidation pathway that generates N A D P H (for reductive biosynthesis) and pentose phosphates (for nucleotide synthesis)
